final_answer:
FINAL_ANSWER: Lightning cannot strike the same place twice in the same instant.

Explanation:
While lightning can indeed strike the same location multiple times during a storm, it cannot strike the same exact location twice in the same instant. This is because lightning is a natural electrical discharge that occurs during a thunderstorm, and the electrical charge that creates the lightning bolt travels through the air at incredibly fast speeds, typically around 270,000 kilometers per second (170,000 miles per second).

When lightning strikes a location, it creates a channel of ionized air called a leader. This leader is incredibly hot and can reach temperatures of up to 30,000 degrees Celsius (54,000 degrees Fahrenheit). The leader travels through the air until it reaches a grounded object, at which point the electrical discharge is completed and the lightning bolt is formed.

However, because the speed of light is finite, the lightning bolt cannot strike the same exact location twice in the same instant. By the time the lightning bolt forms, the leader has already traveled a significant distance through the air, so it would be impossible for the bolt to strike the same location again before it had even reached its destination.

Therefore, while lightning can indeed strike the same location multiple times during a storm, it cannot strike the same exact location twice in the same instant.
